text: We present a case of robotic assisted cortical sparing left adrenalectomy in a patient with von Hippel-Lindau syndrome.
As you can see, it is an autosomal dominant tumor predisposition syndrome affecting multiple organs.
The frequency of pheochromocytomas and paragangliomas in these patients varies between seven and twenty percent.
Among patients who develop pheochromocytomas, up to forty-seven percent will develop bilateral lesions.
Our patient is a twenty-one year old male patient who underwent laparoscopic right adrenalectomy approximately six years ago for pheochromocytoma.
Patient has been following with endocrinologist in order to have recent elevation in urine metanephrines.
MIBG scan showed uptake in the region of the left adrenal gland as shown in this MRI picture with a nodule.
Preoperative preparation involves adrenergic blockade with phenoxybenzamine and propranol.
The patient is positioned in the modified right lateral decubitus position using a beanbag and footboard, preparing for the robotic adrenalectomy procedure.
The assistant surgeon stands on the patient's left side as the robotic cart is positioned at a forty-five-degree angle off the patient's left shoulder, preparing for the adrenalectomy procedure.
The patient is under general endotracheal anesthesia, and the surgeon uses a varice needle to insufflate the abdomen in the left upper quadrant.
The patient is positioned on an airplane table, tilted to the left for trocar placement and then adjusted to the right for the surgical operation.
The patient is positioned in a steep reverse Trendelenburg position to enhance exposure of the left upper quadrant for the adrenalectomy procedure.
Trocar placement is as shown in the picture.
The accessory port, labeled as number four, is a smaller port utilized by the assistant surgeon during the procedure.
During initial laparoscopy, the surgeon observes adhesions between the splenic flexure and the spleen, which are carefully dissected and taken down using a ligature device.
The surgeon visualizes the left kidney after taking down the attachments, with the spleen appearing completely immobilized in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the Gerota's fascia overlying the left kidney and adrenal gland, preparing for further dissection in the left upper quadrant.
The surgeon continues dissection of the fascia to expose the left renal vein, revealing its location and orientation relative to the surrounding anatomical structures.
In the right-hand corner of the surgical field, the left renal vein is identified, and the surgeon dissects the left adrenal vein.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the left adrenal vein, then applies clips to secure it before dividing the vein.
The surgeon dissects and removes the xerotase fascia covering the adrenal gland, revealing the gland beneath.
The surgeon dissects the surrounding tissue to clearly identify the pheochromocytoma, preparing to perform a cortical sparing adrenalectomy.
After removing Girotta's fascia, the adrenal gland is fully exposed, showing no clear demarcation of the pheochromocytoma.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic ultrasound to identify the nodule within the adrenal gland, with the ultrasound image displayed in the video insert.
The robotic system is positioned, and the surgeon marks the location of the adrenal nodule for further dissection.
The surgeon uses hook electrocautery in the right arm of the robotic system to open the cortex of the adrenal gland at the site of the nodule, revealing underlying tissue.
The surgeon uses a gauze piece and a suction irrigator to manage expected bleeding during the dissection of the adrenal gland, ensuring clear visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery in the left arm and hook cautery in the right arm of the robot to dissect outside the pseudocapsule of the identified adrenal nodule.
With improved stability of the robot and three-dimensional visualization, this portion can be completed more precisely with the robot than would be possible laparoscopically.
The surgeon uses robotic arms to perform further dissection around the pseudocapsule, ultimately removing the nodule from the adrenal gland.
The surgeon controls bleeding by applying pressure and using limited electrocautery on the adrenal gland.
The patient was discharged home after a twenty-three hour observation on replacement steroids.
Patient was seen in clinic at two weeks with uneventful recovery.
And at two months, patient is now on half-dose steroids for maintenance.
Our conclusions.
Cortical sparing partial adrenectomy is feasible.
The robotic system offers enhanced three-dimensional visualization of the surgical field, allowing precise manipulation with minimal disturbance to the adjacent normal adrenal tissue.
Thank you.
